summary: DeVault argues that the economics of open source are fundamentally rooted in collaboration, making them incompatible with business models that seek exclusive monetization rights. He examines how startups attracted by open source economics discovered that the model works for the industry as a whole but not necessarily for individual companies seeking sole profit. He critiques emerging 'post-open' and 'fair source' movements as attempts to retain open source's collaborative benefits while restricting commercial rights, which he considers irreconcilable. He respects these movements' right to exist but insists they not co-opt the 'open source' label, and maintains that successful open source businesses must embrace shared commercial markets.
key_points: Open source economics are built on collaboration and shared R&D costs, which fundamentally conflict with exclusive monetization by a single entity
Many startups discovered their open source product's market cap was smaller than their investment, making investor returns impossible under the open source model
Emerging movements like 'fair source' and 'post-open' rely on an 'auteur theory' of software ownership that undermines the collaborative incentives that make open source work
The best funding model for infrastructure software like databases is coalitions of commercial stakeholders co-maintaining the project, not startups
These new movements are welcome to compete with open source but must not redefine or co-opt the open source label
money_quotes: The revolutionary economics of FOSS are based on collaboration, and are incompatible with competition.
No one is incentivized to work for you, for free, for your own exclusive profit.
Open source ate a lot of lunches. There are some kinds of software which you just can't make in a classic silicon valley startup fashion anymore.
If Linux can be said to belong to anyone, it belongs to everyone. It is for this reason that it is in everyone's interests to collaborate on the project.
I think that the aim of sole or limited rights to monetization and the desire to foster a collaborative environment are irreconcilable.
To win in open source you must embrace this collaboration and embrace the fact that you will share the commercial market for the software with other entities.
Simply saying that the software itself is a gift equal to the labor you ask of the public is probably not going to work, especially when this 'gift' comes with monetary strings attached.
key_insight: The collaborative foundation that makes open source economically powerful is fundamentally incompatible with any model that reserves exclusive commercial rights for a single entity.
